mcq.explanation_md: You can never instantiate an abstract class — `new Payment()` asks Java to build an object with a `pay()` method that has no body, which makes no sense, so the compiler refuses. The tempting wrong pick is `Payment p = new Upi();` — it *looks* suspicious because the types differ, but it's not only legal, it's the whole point of abstraction: a parent-type reference holding a concrete child. That's the pattern Spring and every real codebase lean on. `Payment p = null;` is also fine — declaring a reference of an abstract type is allowed; it's only `new` that's forbidden.
